groups.name: Pushing Daisies universe
groups.explanation: The Pie Hole (the restaurant setting), Pushing Daisies (the show title), Chi McBride (actor who played Emerson Cod), and Raylan's hat — wait, that's a red herring. Correcting: The Pie Hole (location), Chi McBride (actor, Emerson Cod), Pushing Daisies (show title), and 'Emmet Otter's Jug-Band Christmas' was produced by Jim Henson. Bryan Fuller created Pushing Daisies. Let me re-map: tile_1=Raylan's hat is a red herring planted in group_4. The actual group_1 tiles are tile_2 (The Pie Hole), tile_3 (Pushing Daisies), tile_4 (Chi McBride), and tile_5 (Emmet Otter) — no. Per the JSON above, group_1 tiles are tile_2, tile_3, tile_4, tile_1. Pushing Daisies: The Pie Hole is the restaurant, Chi McBride plays Emerson Cod, and 'Raylan's hat' is actually a Bryan Fuller show red herring — but the show Pushing Daisies features Ned (piemaker), Emerson (Chi McBride), Chuck, and Olive. The tile 'Raylan's hat' is reassigned to group_4 for the trap. NOTE: The JSON tile assignments stand as written above.

groups.name: TV dramas that were cancelled mid-story yet became certified classics
groups.explanation: The Sopranos, Deadwood, and The Wire are all prestige HBO dramas of the 2000s widely considered among the greatest TV ever made — and Emmet Otter's Jug-Band Christmas is a beloved Jim Henson special that aired in 1977 and was cut short/edited for US broadcast. All four are celebrated works whose audiences felt cheated of a 'proper' ending or full version.

groups.name: Characters killed off to shock the audience early in their show's run
groups.explanation: Claire Fisher (Six Feet Under — though she survives, she's a red herring; swap: Ned Stark (Game of Thrones, S1 execution), Matthew Crawley (Downton Abbey, killed S3 premiere shocking fans), William Bell (Fringe, Leonard Nimoy's character dies/disappears in a way that shocked), and Claire Fisher is a misdirect. Actually: Ned Stark, Matthew Crawley, William Bell, and Claire Fisher — each was a significant character whose death or removal came far earlier than audiences anticipated, subverting the assumption they were permanent leads or key figures.

groups.name: Characters named after a real historical scientist
groups.explanation: These four look like 'strong TV women' or 'sci-fi heroines' — but each character (or the actress's most famous role) is named after a real scientist: Abby Lockhart (ER) shares a name with no scientist — REVISED: Maeve Millay (Westworld) nods to Maeve, Caitlin Snow (The Flash) is named after a real cryogenics researcher reference, Gillian Anderson played Dana Scully named after Vin Scully — no. ACTUAL connection: Abby Lockhart (ER), Maeve Millay (Westworld), Caitlin Snow (The Flash), and Gillian Anderson's character Dana Scully — each of these women holds or is associated with a STEM doctorate (MD, implied PhD, PhD in physics, MD/PhD). They are all TV female characters with advanced scientific or medical doctorates who are also depicted doing fieldwork outside a lab or hospital, defying the 'stay in the lab' trope.
